text: Some time later the king of the Ammonites died, and his son took the throne in his place.

David decided to show kindness to the new king, Hanun, remembering how his father had once shown kindness to him, so he sent envoys to console him over his father's death; David's men traveled to the land of the Ammonites.
But the Ammonite officials told Hanun that David had not sent the men out of respect for his father — they were really spies, sent to scout the city before attacking it.
So Hanun seized David's envoys, shaved off half of each man's beard, cut their robes off at the hip, and sent them away.
When David heard how humiliated the men were, he sent word for them to wait in Jericho until their beards grew back, rather than return to the capital in shame.

Realizing they had angered David badly, the Ammonites paid for reinforcements — Aramean foot soldiers from Beth-rehob and Zobah numbering twenty thousand, a thousand men from the king of Maacah, and twelve thousand men from Tob.
When David learned of it, he sent Joab out with the whole army of experienced fighters.
The Ammonites took their position at the entrance to their city gate, while the hired Arameans of Zobah, Rehob, Tob, and Maacah stood apart in the open country.
Seeing that he would have to fight on two fronts, Joab chose his best troops and arranged them to face the Arameans.
He put the rest of the army under his brother Abishai's command to face the Ammonites.
Joab told his brother that if the Arameans proved too strong for him, Abishai should come to his aid, and if the Ammonites proved too strong for Abishai, he would come to help him.
He urged his men to be brave for the sake of their people and their God's cities, and to trust the LORD to bring about whatever outcome he saw fit.
When Joab and his troops advanced against the Arameans, the Arameans broke and ran.
Seeing the Arameans flee, the Ammonites also fled from Abishai and retreated inside the city; Joab then withdrew from the Ammonites and returned to Jerusalem.
After seeing themselves defeated by Israel, the Arameans regrouped their forces.
Hadadezer sent for Aramean troops from across the Euphrates River, and they came to Helam led by Shobach, the commander of Hadadezer's army.
When David was told of this, he assembled all of Israel, crossed the Jordan, and marched to Helam, where the Arameans lined up to face him.
The Arameans fled before Israel; David's forces killed seven hundred of their charioteers and forty thousand horsemen, and struck down Shobach, the army commander, who died there.
When the kings allied with Hadadezer saw they had been defeated, they made peace with Israel and submitted to David; from then on the Arameans were unwilling to help the Ammonites again.
